Chapter OUtline 9.1 Sequences and Their Notations 9.2 Arithmetic Sequences 9.3 Geometric Sequences 9.4 Series and Their Notations 9.5 Counting Principles 9.6 Binomial Theorem 9.7 Probability Figure 1 (credit: Robert S. Donovan, Flickr.) Introduction A lottery winner has some big decisions to make regarding what to do with the winnings. Buy a villa in Saint Barth├σlemy? A luxury convertible? A cruise around the world? The likelihood of winning the lottery is slim, but we all love to fantasize about what we could buy with the winnings. One of the first things a lottery winner has to decide is whether to take the winnings in the form of a lump sum or as a series of regular payments, called an annuity, over the next 30 years or so. This decision is often based on many factors, such as tax implications, interest rates, and investment strategies. There are also personal reasons to consider when making the choice, and one can make many arguments for either decision. However, most lottery winners opt for the lump sum. In this chapter, we will explore the mathematics behind situations such as these. We will take an in-depth look at annuities. We will also look at the branch of mathematics that would allow us to calculate the number of ways to choose lottery numbers and the probability of winning. Sequences, Probability and Counting Theory

See Example 6, Example 7, and Example 8. έΑλ An annuity is an account into which the investor makes a series of regularly scheduled payments. The value of an annuity can be found using geometric series. See Example 9. 9.5 Counting Principles έΑλ If one event can occur in m ways and a second event with no common outcomes can occur in n ways, then the fi st or second event can occur in m + n ways. See Example 1. έΑλ If one event can occur in m ways and a second event can occur in n ways after the fi st event has occurred, then the two events can occur in m ├Ω n ways. See Example 2. έΑλ A permutation is an ordering of n objects. έΑλ If we have a set of n objects and we want to choose r objects from the set in order, we write P(n, r). έΑλ Permutation problems can be solved using the Multiplication Principle or the formula for P(n, r). See Example 3 and Example 4. έΑλ A selection of objects where the order does not matter is a combination. έΑλ Given n distinct objects, the number of ways to select r objects from the set is C (n, r) and can be found using a formula. See Example 5. έΑλ A set containing n distinct objects has 2n subsets. See Example 6. έΑλ For counting problems involving non-distinct objects, we need to divide to avoid counting duplicate permutations. See Example 7. 9.6 Binomial Theorem έΑλ έΑΜΌΑλ έΑΜ n _ r έΑΜ ΌΑςέΑΜ is called a binomial coeffici t and is equal to C (n, r). See Example 1. έΑλ The Binomial Theorem allows us to expand binomials without multiplying. See Example 2. έΑλ We can fi d a given term of a binomial expansi

Learning Objectives In this section, you will: έΑλ Apply the Binomial Theorem. 9.6 Binomial Theorem A polynomial with two terms is called a binomial. We have already learned to multiply binomials and to raise binomials to powers, but raising a binomial to a high power can be tedious and time-consuming. In this section, we will discuss a shortcut that will allow us to find (x + y)n without multiplying the binomial by itself n times. Identifying Binomial Coefficients In Counting Principles, we studied combinations. In the shortcut to finding (x + y)n, we will need to use combinations to find the coefficients that will appear in the expansion of the binomial. In this case, we use the notation έΑΜΌΑλ έΑΜ n _ r έΑΜ ΌΑςέΑΜ instead of C(n, r), but it can be calculated in the same way. So έΑΜΌΑλ έΑΜ n _ r έΑΜ ΌΑςέΑΜ = C(n, r) = έΑΜ n! ________ r!(n έΙΤ r)! έΑΜ The combination έΑΜΌΑλ έΑΜ n _ r έΑΜ ΌΑςέΑΜ is called a binomial coefficient. An example of a binomial coefficient is έΑΜΌΑλ έΑΜ 5 __ 2 έΑΜ ΌΑςέΑΜ = C(5, 2) = 10. binomial coefficients If n and r are integers greater than or equal to 0 with n έΚξ r, then the binomial coefficient is έΑΜΌΑλ έΑΜ n _ r έΑΜ ΌΑςέΑΜ = C(n, r) = έΑΜ n! ________ r!(n έΙΤ r)! έΑΜ Q & AέΑο Is a binomial coefficient always a whole number? Yes. Just as the number of combinations must always be a whole number, a binomial coefficient will always be a whole number. Example 1 Finding Binomial Coefficients Find each binomial coefficient. a. έΑΜΌΑλ έΑΜ 5 __ 3 έΑΜ ΌΑςέΑΜ b. έΑΜΌΑλ έΑΜ 9 __ 2 έΑΜ ΌΑςέΑΜ c. έΑΜΌΑλ έΑΜ 9 __ 7 έΑΜ ΌΑςέΑΜ Solution Use the formula to calculate each binomial coefficient. You can also use the nCr function on your calculator. έΑΜΌΑλ έΑΜ n _ r έΑΜ ΌΑςέΑΜ = C(n,

How ToέΑο Given a binomial, write a specific term without fully expanding. 1. Determine the value of n according to the exponent. 2. Determine (r + 1). 3. Determine r. 4. Replace r in the formula for the (r + 1)th term of the binomial expansion. Example 3 Writing a Given Term of a Binomial Expansion Find the tenth term of (x + 2y)16 without fully expanding the binomial. Solution Because we are looking for the tenth term, r + 1 = 10, we will use r = 9 in our calculations. έΑΜΌΑλ έΑΜ n _ r έΑΜ ΌΑςέΑΜxn έΙΤ ryr έΑΜΌΑλ έΑΜ 16 _ 9 έΑΜ ΌΑςέΑΜx16 έΙΤ 9(2y)9 = 5,857,280x7y 9 Try It #3 Find the sixth term of (3x έΙΤ y)9 without fully expanding the binomial. Access these online resources for additional instruction and practice with binomial expansion. έΑλ The Binomial Theorem (http://openstaxcollege.org/l/binomialtheorem) έΑλ Binomial Theorem Example (http://openstaxcollege.org/l/btexample)
